  1. Is this acceptable to reviewers in reputable journals?

  2. How should I address this in my SLR?

  3. Is it appropriate to email the authors for copies of their papers?

The Global International Journal of Innovative Research has published a fake research article under my name, complete with a DOI doi:10.59613/global.v1i3.42. The article appears to be entirely AI-generated. I never had anything to do with the article: I never created the text, nor did I submit it for publication. I assume the journal has created and published the article to lend credence to its title.

The article's publication is disparaging me by associating my name with the article's drivel, the journal publishing such nonsense, and the unethical practice of publishing AI-generated text without appropriate acknowledgements. It seems that the article is gathering attention. I became aware of it, when a colleague fell for it and found it relevant to one of our department's initiatives. What can I do?

Update 2025-05-30: I have now published an article presenting my analysis of the journal's content and discussing the practice's effect and the possible countermeasures:

Diomidis Spinellis. False authorship: An explorative case study around an AI-generated article published under my name. Research Integrity and Peer Review, 10(1), May 2025. doi:10.1186/s41073-025-00165-z.

I teach undergraduate mathematics. This semester, I started walking around the classroom while using an iPad to write (to the screen) during lectures. This allows me to notice that a few students are not paying attention and doing all sorts of things on their laptops, sometimes even watching YouTube videos right in front of me. I wonder what the best approach would be to handle such students. I see a few options:

  1. Completely ignore them and focus on students who are engaged and there to study.
  2. Be more direct and ask them to answer questions in class.
  3. Invite them to office hours to understand why they attend class but do not engage. (Note: I do not enforce attendance.)

What would you suggest based on your experience?

For most academics, it is professionally vital to stay current in our fields. However, the volume of publication appears to be increasing exponentially [1, 2]. The first of these studies estimates a doubling time of 15 years.

What are good strategies for coping with this increasing volume of literature?

I need to make a PRISMA systematic literature review on a sociological topic. To do this, I need to systematically search the major databases, arriving at a list of metadata of the academic papers (articles, book chapters) that fit the search terms. For now, the important thing is finding this metadata in a systematic way (i.e., not just cobbling together references from whatever sources I happen to think of). Finding the full-text of the papers is not (yet) the issue.

The problem is that I am in Russia, and we do not have access to Web of Science and Scopus even via my university. For example, WoS search by documents is prohibited even with VPN.

Given this, how can academics in countries like Russia complete a systematic literature review? Are there known strategies for overcoming this problem?

I am teaching a course and classes are over. I have already created and printed the final exam. One of my students emailed me with a question that happens to be identical to one of the questions in the exam.

I am unsure how to handle this situation. Should I respond to the student's email, knowing that it might give them an advantage and potentially be unfair to other students? Or would it be better to ignore the email entirely to maintain fairness?

What is the best course of action in such a scenario?

I've recently reviewed a paper in a prestigious journal in my field, which was very poor and got rejected. In my reviewer report, I listed the errors and unsubstantiated claims that the authors have made, to at least give them a chance to improve in their next submission. I've seen the reports of the other reviewers pointing out the same issues. Not so long after, I saw the paper published in one of MDPI's journals, to my horror, with no corrections made to the issues that I pointed out.

This experience made me mistrust the "peer-review" process of predatory journals, as well as mistrust authors who choose to go to such journals. However, there are research groups that work in areas close to mine that continue to publish that way (if only the authors of the said paper!). Their research is, technically, close to mine, which could justify mentioning in literature reviews or in my own papers. Their findings or conclusions could, technically, provide additional arguments to motivate my work in grant proposals that I write. But how can I trust those findings or conclusions if they haven't been properly peer-reviewed? FWIW, they may be totally fabricated.

In my own research, grant writing, etc., is it an ethical decision to ignore papers published in predatory journals, even if they are related to my research?

Edit:

My question is quite different from this one as it goes beyond just citing. For example, as this answer states, it's clear to me that if I used a research finding in any way, I should cite the source. It's clear to me that this is an ethical thing to do. A more precise version of my question is this: Can I, ethically, not even spend time to familiarize myself with research that is published in a journal listed as predatory (say here or here) even if the title and the authors list suggest that this paper does something close to my own research?

Imagine this scenario: I get a Google Scholar notification about "Recommended articles" for me. There, I see a title that sounds like research close to mine, I know the authors are from my field, but the publisher is predatory. If I decide to never click on that link and look into that paper (assuming it may be flawed and a waste of my time), is this ethical? Or will I be doing science a disservice?

I’m a research librarian exploring the potential to offer freelance support for a variety of scholarly reviews, especially systematic and scoping reviews, but also narrative and other reviews too, and including search strategy design, database selection, doing the actual searches, compiling articles to be used for the inclusion/exclusion phase, and method documentation.

My goal is to provide independent, research librarian support that includes some or all of:

  • Collaborating on research question development

  • Designing and running search strategies across multiple databases

  • Organizing results, deduplicating, and exporting to citation tools

  • Documenting methods in detail, including writing the methods section related to search design and strategy

This would mirror the kind of contribution librarians often provide on institutional teams, where they are frequently credited as co-authors or collaborators, but offered independently for those who may not have access to that kind of support, which can vary a lot by institution.

Has anyone seen demand for this kind of freelance work in academic settings? Are there barriers I should be aware of when offering this outside of an institutional affiliation?

I'm a lecturer in courses with 120-150 students. At my university, there are four months of lectures, and after that, students can take the exam. There are no midterms, mid-projects, or evaluations midway.

The students have 7 exam sessions. If they pass the exam in one of those sessions, then they pass the course (they need to take only one exam, not all seven; they have seven possibilities to take it). After they take an exam session, their grades are published, and they can review the exam to check for errors they have made. Then, they can decide whether to accept or reject the grade. If they accept it, the grade gets recorded in their transcript. If they reject it, they can take a different exam (either one of the other 6 sessions, or wait until the next academic year to get 7 more opportunities). Students need to enroll for each exam in advance using the university's website.

In order to manage those exams, in the first lecture I present them with the exam schedule, which may go something like that:

Exam n. 1

Enrollment begins: 2025-11-02
Enrollment ends: 2025-11-12
Exam: 2025-11-15
Publication: 2025-11-25
Grades review: 2025-11-26
Grades recording: 2025-11-28

Despite this, at each exam I receive emails after the enrollment deadline telling me that "I couldn't enroll", "I was not able to enroll", "The website didn't work" (and you waited until after the deadline to report that the website didn't work?) and ask weird questions like "is there the possibility to enroll" or "how can I check whether am I enrolled" or "can I come to the exam"? I think they expect me to enroll them manually.

If I say "yes", then 20 seconds later I receive 20 other e-mails telling me "I too had problems!", "I too couldn't enroll!", and if I reply "no" to those, they say "Why not? Why my colleague got enrolled manually and I don't get to enroll manually? The rules are the same for everybody!!! The exam procedures are the same for everybody!!!". And I really don't and shouldn't waste my time enrolling people manually and answering such emails.

If I say "no" right from the beginning, they say, "Why not? What does it cost you? What changes does it make to you? Professor X enrolled me manually. Why did Professor X enroll me manually, and you don't? In Germany, people are enrolled manually. Why are people in Germany enrolled manually, and here they aren't? Then I do an oral examination. Why can't I do an oral examination? Then I do the exam at home and send it to you by email. Why can't I do the exam by email? Then I come anyway, and if there are spots, I sit, and if there are no spots, I stand. Why can't I do the exam standing?" and so on. And I really don't want to answer all those questions (I don't know the answer to many of them anyway).

A few days ago, after the 5th exam, I received an email with "Urgent! Urgent!" as subject and the student telling me he has scholarships and housing and he needs the grade recorded "as soon as possible" (they never have a deadline, it's always "before tomorrow, on second thought, before today, on second thought, as soon as possible").

I think he should have done one of the 4 previous exams rather than waiting for the 5th one and then send e-mails with "Urgent! Urgent!" as subject.

How can I manage this situation? I have a lot of research duties, and I need a precise schedule for teaching. Because currently, I'm just blocking them. There are no university-wide rules for handling this sort of thing; each instructor can use their discretion.

I had a PhD student from 2022 to 2024, who from the get-go was just not a good fit. I accepted her into my group, gave her funding, only to realize quite quickly that she was really not a good presenter, would get very rudimentary things wrong, repeated herself a lot when talking, asking a lot of clarifying questions during seminars without researching them herself, etc.

I pulled her aside in early 2023, and told her directly that she wasn't going to graduate. She seemed to be swallowing back tears, but said thank you for the feedback, and kept showing up, but she wasn't putting in the effort anymore. She would disappear for days if not weeks at a time, claiming "her boyfriend was going through something bad" or "she was having personal health issues". She opened up to me about some things that honestly were none of my business, and I am not sure if she was seeking sympathy or what.

She resigned in 2024 and told me she was moving back to her home country to do some very basic manual job, saying she wasn't "good enough" for anything else, with a lot of self-pity. I was okay with her resigning. Did not hear from her for quite a while.

Then, in 2025, she came to the university, and had somehow convinced an events organizer to make arrangements to give a speech about "alternative mathematics career paths outside of academia". There, she all of a sudden spoke like someone I hadn't seen before. She was still in the country, living here. Hadn't left. The whole speech was about how she set up her business, and started making money. A lot of money. During the speech, she maintained this weird eye contact with me the whole time, then excitedly answered questions. She was like a completely different person.

Okay, maybe not too strange, but then odd things began happening.

Her dad, who is about my age, is a dean at another university, working in an adjacent research area to mine, which I visited in 2025. During my visit then, she was again there. In a different country. She and her dad both kept glaring at me during my visit, and would ignore me. Her dad didn't even shake my hand, despite seemingly greeting everyone else in this manner. These odd things where I would "run into her" started happening a lot in 2025. These two mentioned above were not the only two instances of this happening. Here are some other ones: She secured a teaching position with a colleague of mine at a different university, and despite her pursuing a completely different career now, they began doing research together, and uploaded a manuscript lately of a problem that I had initially shared with her. This specific colleague has then distanced themselves from me.

She put together a small conference at the university I work in to talk about "addiction in academia". I didn't attend, but heard from a student of mine writing a master's thesis with me that she kept openly sharing about her struggles, name-dropping my research group, and how she was "belittled" and "her addiction wasn't taken seriously" and hinted that she was pressured to drink. None of this was true.

I would also bump into her in the restaurants near the university all the time, and she would just pretend as if I wasn't there, or glare at me. Either or.

Now, 2026 got even stranger, and she has now committed two acts that have made me want to file a police report with the police here in Germany.

First, my daughter, who is a few years younger than my former student, put together an art show in the city. Guess who was there? Yes, my former PhD student. She kept talking with my daughter and introduced herself to me by shaking my hand as if we had never met before, with words "Hello, so nice to meet you, my name is XXX". My daughter said that they met in the same art community in the city, and have been "chatting about art". The other instance is even worse. I had no idea, but my former student is apparently from quite a wealthy family. Point here being, she and a few whom I assume to be family members of hers, have now bought an apartment in my apartment complex. The city is huge. There is no way this is a coincidence. I found this out by finding her surname on one of the mailboxes. The person living in one of the apartments, whom I've spoken to, is a man about her age who has the same surname, who confirmed that they purchased the property together when I asked.

It all sounds like I am absolutely losing my mind, but I do not think that all of this put together, these incidents are coincidences anymore. I need to take some steps towards doing something about this.

Of course, in such a stalking situation, one should inform someone, but in fact, nothing that she has done is a crime. I doubt anyone will take this seriously, but a paper trail wouldn't be a bad idea.

Just sharing this here in case you can help me. Any and all advice is very welcome.

Without going into too much detail, I was homeless for much of high school and the summer after first year. I confided in the wrong person and, unfortunately, it seems almost all of the undergraduates and some graduate students in my department (STEM at an ‘elite’ R1 university with generally privileged students; about 80 undergrads and as many grad students) now know.

I’ll be a senior in the fall and will likely stay at the same college for a PhD, given the general funding situation in the country. Academically, things have gone well; I’ve done well in research and have generally earned respect in the department. As such, I've thankfully avoided comments about being a 'diversity hire' or 'affirmative action admit'.

However, it seems most compliments from peers are framed in terms of my background, e.g. “good job, especially considering your circumstances” or, in one especially egregious comment, “you’re pretty smart for someone who was homeless."

I don’t think people mean this maliciously, but I feel reduced to “the formerly homeless kid” rather than just another student.

How should I navigate this professionally? Is this something that tends to fade with accomplishment, or is it worth addressing more directly with peers? Would it be inappropriate to directly tell people I dislike these comments? How would faculty view this kind of commentary? It's a small enough department, especially for my specific field, that I'm worried about handling things wrong and so I feel tempted to just ignore this.
